label: Concept to practice
how_joined: This bridge joins Chapter 4 and Chapter 5 by showing how educator meanings of discipline become practical control work. Chapter 4 identifies discipline as obedience, authority, and legitimacy. Chapter 5 shows those meanings being enacted through standards, supervision, accountability, and consequence.
specific_relations: The specific relation is from obedience threshold, hierarchy authority, and authority legitimacy intent in Chapter 4 to standard setting, accountability orientation, consequence linkage, and visibility supervision in Chapter 5. The bridge therefore marks the move from what discipline is said to mean to how it is made visible and enforceable in daily programme life.

label: Practice to experience
how_joined: This bridge joins Chapter 5 and Chapter 6 by showing how operational control becomes youth experience. Chapter 5 traces control through the body, time, correction, and symbolic pressure. Chapter 6 shows how youths could receive those practices as bodily danger, exhaustion, psychological wounding, humiliation, and broken time.
specific_relations: The specific relation is from bodily regulation, bodily restriction, temporal control, coercive correction, and symbolic coercion in Chapter 5 to bodily harm danger, depletion exhaustion, psychological wounding, humiliation display, and temporal disruption in Chapter 6. The bridge therefore marks the route by which disciplinary practice becomes lived strain.

label: Formation and re-reading
how_joined: This bridge joins Chapter 4 and Chapter 6 by showing how educator claims about internalisation and character formation are partly re-read in youth accounts. Chapter 4 frames discipline as something that should outlast supervision. Chapter 6 shows that some youths could later read parts of the programme as routine, respect, positive change, or training with purpose.
specific_relations: The specific relation is from self-regulation autonomy, repetition habit memory, trust credibility, values character carrier, and widened life governance in Chapter 4 to routine internalisation, respect recognition, positive transformation, reframing as training, and principle purpose orientation in Chapter 6. The bridge therefore marks the partial link between intended formation and later youth interpretation.
